questions.question: Tell me about a time you led a major change initiative.
questions.text: Leading the AI-driven warehouse transformation at a Fortune 500 distributor was equal parts vision and grit — 2,400 staff, a legacy WMS, and a 90-day go-live window. We deployed Microsoft Copilot Studio alongside D365 and Manhattan DFIO, automating 90% of tier-1 IT queries almost overnight. The secret wasn't the technology — it was the change management framework that got every stakeholder from skeptic to champion before a single line of code shipped. End result: $2.4M in operational cost savings in year one.
questions.imagePrompt: enterprise change management transformation dashboard with adoption metrics

questions.question: Describe a situation where you had to overcome a significant technical challenge.
questions.text: Integrating Azure OpenAI with a 30-year-old IBM AS400 system isn't in any textbook — we had to build custom middleware that translated EBCDIC flat files into structured API payloads in real time. The hard part wasn't the code; it was convincing the business that the 6-week delay for the integration layer was worth it versus a quick-and-dirty hack. We held the line, built it right, and the system now processes 68% fewer manual orders. Legacy and AI can coexist — you just need a bridge.

chart.title: Manual Order Processing: Before vs After AI Integration

questions.imagePrompt: legacy IBM AS400 mainframe connected to modern Azure OpenAI cloud architecture

questions.question: What is RAG and when would you use it?
questions.text: RAG — Retrieval-Augmented Generation — is the pattern of grounding an LLM's output in real, retrievable data rather than relying on what it memorized during training. You index your source documents into a vector store, retrieve the most semantically relevant chunks at query time, and inject them into the prompt before the model generates its answer. I'd reach for RAG over fine-tuning whenever the data changes frequently (inventory, pricing, policies) or when you need citations and auditability — both non-negotiable in enterprise supply chain contexts. For static, stylistic behavior changes, fine-tuning wins; for dynamic knowledge, RAG is king.

chart.title: RAG vs Fine-Tuning: When to Use Each

questions.imagePrompt: RAG retrieval augmented generation architecture diagram with vector database and LLM

questions.question: How do you evaluate and monitor LLM performance in production?
questions.text: Production LLM monitoring is a different beast from traditional ML — you're tracking latency, token costs, hallucination rate, and user satisfaction simultaneously. I instrument every inference call with structured logging (model, prompt hash, token count, latency, user rating) and pipe it into a dashboard with alert thresholds on p95 latency and cost-per-query. For quality, I use a combination of LLM-as-judge (a second model evaluates outputs against a rubric) and periodic human review of flagged responses. On the Copilot Studio deployment I manage, we dropped the escalation rate from 100% to 10% in three months — that's the metric that tells you the model is actually working.

chart.title: LLM Production Quality Metrics Over Time

questions.imagePrompt: LLM production monitoring dashboard with latency charts and quality metrics

questions.question: How do I write better prompts for ChatGPT?
questions.text: The single biggest upgrade you can make to your prompts is adding role + context + constraint — tell the model who it is, what you need, and what you don't want. Instead of 'summarize this', try 'You are a senior analyst. Summarize this 10-page report into 5 bullet points for a CFO. Omit technical jargon.' Chain-of-thought prompting (adding 'think step by step') dramatically improves accuracy on reasoning tasks. And if you want consistent output format, show an example — models are world-class pattern matchers. On the enterprise deployments I run, prompt templates with these principles cut hallucination rates by over 40% compared to free-form queries.

chart.title: Prompt Technique Impact on Output Quality
